species.citation: A.DC. , Prodr. (A. P. de Candolle) 8 (1844) 237 ; Ng, Tree Fl. Sabah & Sarawak 4 (2002) 42; Villanueva et al., Biodiversitas 23 (2022) 6496. = Diospyros discolor Willd. , Sp. Pl., 4th Ed. 4 (1805) 1108, nom. illeg. ; Merr., Fl. Manila (1912) 364; Merrill, EPFP 3 (1923) 291; Bakh., Bull. Jard. Bot. Buitenz. 3, 15 (1936) 145. = Diospyros philippensis (Desr.) Gürke , Nat. Pflanzenfam. (Engler & Prantl) 4(1) (1890) 164, nom. illeg.; ≡ Cavanillea philippensis Desr. , Encycl. (Lamarck) 3 (1792) 663. = Diospyros merrillii Elmer , LPB 5 (1913) 1775; Type: Elmer 12895 (L, NY, US, iso), Palawan: Palawan, Puerto Princesa, Mt Pulgar (Thumb Peak), in deep soil of densely forested flat at 250ft, Mar-1911.
species.distribution: Borneo, Philippines. BATAN, CAMOTES, CATANDUANES, GUIMARAS, LAGEN, LEYTE, LUZON: Albay, Aurora, Bataan, Bulacan, Cagayan, Camarines, Camarines Sur, Laguna, NCR, Pampanga, Quezon, Rizal, Sorsogon, Zambales, MINDANAO: Agusan del Norte, Davao Oriental, Surigao, Zamboanga del Norte (photos), MINDORO, PALAWAN, PANAY, SAMAR.
species.notes: P.B. Pelser (11-Mar-23): In 1806, the name Diospyros discolor Willd. was published, but this was a superfluous name (Knapp & Gilbert 2002), because Willdenow cited "Cavanillea philippensis Lam.” (referring to Cavanillea philippensis Desr. in Lam.) and a new combination should therefore have been made with the name Diospyros philippensis. When that combination was finally made by Gürke in 1890, a different species was already named Diospyros philippinensis A.DC. (1844). Those two names (i.e. Diospyros philippensis (Desr.) Gürke and Diospyros philippinensis A.DC.) were considered too similar to each other and were therefore regarded homonyms by the ICBN (Barrie 2006). So, a new name in Diospyros had to be found for Cavanillea philippensis Desr. Diospyros mabolo Roxb. ex J.V.Thomps. cannot be used, because it a superfluous name in Diospyros for Cavanillea philippensis Desr. in Lam. Diospyros mabolo (Poir.) Roxb. ex Lindl. (1828) could also not be used, because even though I don’t think there is anything wrong with its basionym (Cavanillea mabolo Poir.; 1794), a note in IPNI suggests that it is an illegitimate name, because it only has an indirect reference to the basionym. The next available name is then Diospyros blancoi A.DC. (1844) and I think that this name is therefore the correct name of this species in Diospyros. In most or all islands and provinces; introduced in other tropical countries. Primary and secondary forests at low and medium elevation, including limestone. Also commonly planted in and about towns for its large and edible fruits, called kamagong or mabolo. 76m to 100-200m (photos).

species.distribution: C & S America. BALABAC, LUZON, PALAWAN, PANAY (photos).
species.notes: A Mexican species introduced in the Philippines and escaped from cultivation and became wild. Widely cultivated in the Philippines in and about towns for its large edible fruits.
